title: Der Wolf und die sieben jungen Geislein
titleEn: The Wolf and the Seven Young Kids
part: The Wolf and the Seven Kids
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 5. Text from Wikisource (proofread against the original scans). 1857 spelling preserved exactly, including the ß-less Geis/Geislein/Geiserchen, gieng/fieng/anfieng, frißt, thun/thust/gethan, Thier, todt, rauh, Scheere and Hausthür.
about: The fifth tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 5), and one of the great “beast in disguise” stories. A mother goat warns her seven kids against the wolf, who tricks his way in by disguising his rough voice with chalk and his black paw with dough and flour; the youngest, hidden in the clock-case, survives to help the mother cut the others alive from the sleeping wolf’s belly and fill it with stones. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so the goats are Geis and Geislein (not the modern Geiß/Geißlein), and you will meet older forms such as gieng, frißt, thun, Thier and todt.

sentences.t: Es war einmal eine alte Geis, die hatte sieben junge Geislein, und hatte sie lieb, wie eine Mutter ihre Kinder lieb hat.
sentences.l: There was once an old nanny-goat, who had seven young little-goats, and had them dear, as a mother has her children dear.
sentences.i: There was once an old nanny-goat who had seven little kids, and she loved them as a mother loves her children.
sentences.n: The classic fairy-tale opening, “Es war einmal” = “Once upon a time.” Note the 1857 spelling Geis (modern Geiß, a nanny-goat) and its diminutive Geislein (modern Geißlein, a kid); Grimm’s tales are full of such -lein / -chen diminutives — you’ll also meet Geiserchen and Mütterchen here. “lieb haben” = to hold dear, to love. The relative “die hatte” resumes the goat: German often points back to a noun with a demonstrative die/der/das.

sentences.t: Eines Tages wollte sie in den Wald gehen und Futter holen, da rief sie alle sieben herbei und sprach „liebe Kinder, ich will hinaus in den Wald, seid auf eurer Hut vor dem Wolf, wenn er herein kommt, so frißt er Euch alle mit Haut und Haar.
sentences.l: One day she wanted to go into the forest and fetch fodder, then she called all seven hither and spoke “dear children, I want out into the forest, be on your guard against the wolf, when he comes in, then he devours you all with skin and hair.
sentences.i: One day she wanted to go into the forest and fetch fodder, so she called all seven to her and said: “Dear children, I’m going out into the forest; be on your guard against the wolf — if he gets in, he’ll eat you all up, skin and hair and all.
sentences.n: “eines Tages” = “one day” (a genitive of time). fressen (here frißt, with the 1857 ß) is the verb for an animal eating — it carries a devouring, greedy sense that essen (for people) does not. “auf der Hut sein” = to be on one’s guard; “mit Haut und Haar” (“with skin and hair”) is a set phrase = completely, every last bit.

sentences.t: Der Bösewicht verstellt sich oft, aber an seiner rauhen Stimme und an seinen schwarzen Füßen werdet ihr ihn gleich erkennen.“
sentences.l: The villain disguises himself often, but by his rough voice and by his black feet you will recognize him at once.”
sentences.i: The villain often disguises himself, but you’ll know him at once by his rough voice and his black feet.”
sentences.n: “sich verstellen” = to disguise oneself, put on an act (reflexive). rauh is the 1857 spelling of modern rau (rough, harsh) — the h stays on through the whole tale (rauhen). These two tells, the rough voice and the black paws, drive the whole plot.

sentences.t: Die Geislein sagten, „liebe Mutter, wir wollen uns schon in Acht nehmen, Ihr könnt ohne Sorge fortgehen.“
sentences.l: The little-goats said, “dear mother, we will surely take ourselves into heed, you can go away without worry.”
sentences.i: The kids said: “Dear mother, we’ll take good care; you can go without a worry.”
sentences.n: “sich in Acht nehmen” = to take care, be on one’s guard (a fixed idiom). schon here isn’t “already” but a reassuring “surely, don’t worry.” Ihr (capitalized) is the old respectful “you” the children use to their mother — a polite plural, like French vous. fortgehen (to go away) is separable.

sentences.t: Da meckerte die Alte und machte sich getrost auf den Weg.
sentences.l: Then the old one bleated and made herself, reassured, on the way.
sentences.i: Then the old goat bleated and set off with an easy mind.
sentences.n: meckern is the verb for a goat’s bleat. “sich auf den Weg machen” = to set out, get going (reflexive idiom). getrost = reassured, with an untroubled mind. die Alte = “the old one,” i.e. the mother goat.

sentences.t: Es dauerte nicht lange, so klopfte jemand an die Hausthür und rief „macht auf, ihr lieben Kinder, eure Mutter ist da und hat jedem von Euch etwas mitgebracht.“
sentences.l: It lasted not long, then someone knocked at the house-door and called “open up, you dear children, your mother is here and has brought each of you something.”
sentences.i: It wasn’t long before someone knocked at the front door and called: “Open up, dear children, your mother is here and has brought each of you something.”
sentences.n: Hausthür = modern Haustür (front door); the th- is the 1857 spelling, which you’ll see again in Thüre, Thier, thun. aufmachen (to open) is separable, so the prefix jumps off in the imperative: “macht auf.” mitbringen (to bring along) is separable too: “hat… mitgebracht.”

sentences.t: Aber die Geiserchen hörten an der rauhen Stimme daß es der Wolf war, „wir machen nicht auf,“ riefen sie, „du bist unsere Mutter nicht, die hat eine feine und liebliche Stimme, aber deine Stimme ist rauh; du bist der Wolf.“
sentences.l: But the little-goats heard by the rough voice that it was the wolf, “we open not up,” they called, “you are our mother not, she has a fine and lovely voice, but your voice is rough; you are the wolf.”
sentences.i: But the little goats could tell by the rough voice that it was the wolf. “We won’t open up,” they cried; “you’re not our mother — she has a fine, sweet voice, but your voice is rough; you’re the wolf.”
sentences.n: Geiserchen is another diminutive of Geis — “the little goats,” interchangeable here with Geislein. daß (the 1857 spelling of modern dass) simply marks a “that”-clause and sends its verb to the end (“daß es der Wolf war”). Their first defense: the voice.

sentences.t: Da gieng der Wolf fort zu einem Krämer, und kaufte sich ein großes Stück Kreide: die aß er und machte damit seine Stimme fein.
sentences.l: Then the wolf went away to a shopkeeper, and bought himself a large piece of chalk: that he ate and made with it his voice fine.
sentences.i: So the wolf went off to a shopkeeper and bought himself a big piece of chalk; he ate it and made his voice fine that way.
sentences.n: gieng is the 1857 spelling of modern ging (went) — the old -ie- turns up throughout this tale (fieng, anfieng). fortgehen is separable: “gieng… fort.” “die aß er” — die points back to the chalk (“that he ate”), a demonstrative fronted for emphasis. The chalk is the folk remedy for a rough voice.

sentences.t: Dann kam er zurück, klopfte an die Hausthür und rief „macht auf, ihr lieben Kinder, eure Mutter ist da und hat jedem von Euch etwas mitgebracht.“
sentences.l: Then came he back, knocked at the house-door and called “open up, you dear children, your mother is here and has brought each of you something.”
sentences.i: Then he came back, knocked at the front door and called: “Open up, dear children, your mother is here and has brought each of you something.”
sentences.n: The wolf repeats the lure word for word — the tale’s threefold structure (three attempts at the door) is built on this repetition. zurückkommen (to come back) is separable: “kam… zurück.”

sentences.t: Aber der Wolf hatte seine schwarze Pfote in das Fenster gelegt, das sahen die Kinder und riefen „wir machen nicht auf, unsere Mutter hat keinen schwarzen Fuß, wie du: du bist der Wolf.“
sentences.l: But the wolf had laid his black paw in the window, that saw the children and called “we open not up, our mother has no black foot, like you: you are the wolf.”
sentences.i: But the wolf had laid his black paw on the windowsill; the children saw it and cried: “We won’t open up — our mother has no black foot like yours; you’re the wolf.”
sentences.n: Pfote is a paw (Fuß is the foot in the children’s reply). “das sahen die Kinder” — again a fronted das (“that the children saw”), which throws the verb ahead of the subject. Their second defense: the black paw.

sentences.t: Da lief der Wolf zu einem Bäcker und sprach „ich habe mich an den Fuß gestoßen, streich mir Teig darüber.“
sentences.l: Then ran the wolf to a baker and spoke “I have knocked myself on the foot, spread me dough over it.”
sentences.i: So the wolf ran to a baker and said: “I’ve hurt my foot — spread some dough over it for me.”
sentences.n: “sich (an etwas) stoßen” = to knock/bump oneself (reflexive). streich is the imperative of streichen (to spread, smear); “streich mir… darüber” — the mir is a dative (“for me / on me”). The wolf’s lie sets up the dough-and-flour disguise.

sentences.t: Und als ihm der Bäcker die Pfote bestrichen hatte, so lief er zum Müller und sprach „streu mir weißes Mehl auf meine Pfote.“
sentences.l: And when the baker had spread-over the paw for him, then ran he to the miller and spoke “strew me white flour on my paw.”
sentences.i: And when the baker had coated his paw, he ran on to the miller and said: “Sprinkle white flour on my paw for me.”
sentences.n: bestreichen = to spread something over, coat. streu is the imperative of streuen (to strew, sprinkle). The old resumptive “als…, so…” (“when…, then…”) is a rhythm you’ll meet often in Grimm; modern German drops the so.

sentences.t: Der Müller dachte „der Wolf will einen betrügen“ und weigerte sich, aber der Wolf sprach „wenn du es nicht thust, so fresse ich dich.“
sentences.l: The miller thought “the wolf wants to deceive one” and refused himself, but the wolf spoke “if you it not do, then devour I you.”
sentences.i: The miller thought, “The wolf means to trick somebody,” and refused; but the wolf said: “If you don’t do it, I’ll eat you.”
sentences.n: thust is the 1857 spelling of tust (you do), from thun = modern tun; the th- returns in gethan and the noun Thier. einen here is an indefinite “one / somebody” (accusative). “sich weigern” = to refuse (reflexive). Note fressen again — the wolf devours; the threat is literal.

sentences.t: Da fürchtete sich der Müller und machte ihm die Pfote weiß.
sentences.l: Then feared the miller himself and made the paw white for him.
sentences.i: Then the miller grew afraid and made his paw white.
sentences.n: “sich fürchten” = to be afraid (reflexive). “machte ihm die Pfote weiß” — the ihm is a dative (“for him / his”): German prefers this to a possessive, saying literally “made the paw white to him.”

sentences.t: Ja, das sind die Menschen.
sentences.l: Yes, that are the people.
sentences.i: Yes — that’s people for you.
sentences.n: A dry aside from the narrator: baker and miller both help the wolf out of profit or fear, and Grimm shrugs, “that’s people.” German says “das sind” (“that are”) with a plural verb agreeing with die Menschen.

sentences.t: Nun gieng der Bösewicht zum drittenmal zu der Hausthüre, klopfte an und sprach „macht mir auf, Kinder, euer liebes Mütterchen ist heim gekommen und hat jedem von Euch etwas aus dem Walde mitgebracht.“
sentences.l: Now went the villain for the third-time to the house-door, knocked on and spoke “open up for me, children, your dear little-mother has come home and has brought each of you something out of the forest.”
sentences.i: Now the villain went to the front door a third time, knocked, and said: “Open up for me, children — your dear little mother has come home and has brought each of you something from the forest.”
sentences.n: “zum drittenmal” = for the third time (drittes Mal). Mütterchen is a tender diminutive of Mutter (“mother dear, little mother”). anklopfen (to knock) is separable: “klopfte an.” This is the third and successful attempt — the fairy tale’s magic number.

sentences.t: Die Geiserchen riefen „zeig uns erst deine Pfote, damit wir wissen daß du unser liebes Mütterchen bist.“
sentences.l: The little-goats called “show us first your paw, so-that we know that you are our dear little-mother.”
sentences.i: The little goats called out: “First show us your paw, so we know you’re our dear little mother.”
sentences.n: zeig is the imperative of zeigen (to show). damit = “so that” (purpose), and it sends its verb to the end (“damit wir wissen”). The kids have learned caution — but the flour has beaten them.

sentences.t: Da legte er die Pfote ins Fenster, und als sie sahen daß sie weiß war, so glaubten sie es wäre alles wahr, was er sagte, und machten die Thüre auf.
sentences.l: Then laid he the paw in the window, and when they saw that it was white, then believed they it were all true, what he said, and opened the door up.
sentences.i: Then he laid his paw on the windowsill, and when they saw that it was white, they believed everything he said was true, and opened the door.
sentences.n: “es wäre alles wahr” — wäre is subjunctive (Konjunktiv II), marking what the kids believed rather than plain fact; German slips into this subjunctive for reported belief and speech, and you’ll see it again below (gekommen wäre, gefressen hätte). Thüre = modern Tür (1857 th-). aufmachen separable once more: “machten… auf.”

sentences.t: Wer aber hereinkam, das war der Wolf.
sentences.l: Who however came in, that was the wolf.
sentences.i: But the one who came in — that was the wolf.
sentences.n: “Wer…, das…” is a “whoever…, that…” correlative: the wer-clause sets up a person, and das points back to it. hereinkommen (to come in) is separable, here fused in the clause as hereinkam.

sentences.t: Sie erschraken und wollten sich verstecken.
sentences.l: They took-fright and wanted to hide themselves.
sentences.i: They were terrified and tried to hide.
sentences.n: erschrecken (to be startled, take fright) has the strong past erschraken. “sich verstecken” = to hide oneself (reflexive).

sentences.t: Das eine sprang unter den Tisch, das zweite ins Bett, das dritte in den Ofen, das vierte in die Küche, das fünfte in den Schrank, das sechste unter die Waschschüssel, das siebente in den Kasten der Wanduhr.
sentences.l: The one sprang under the table, the second into the bed, the third into the oven, the fourth into the kitchen, the fifth into the cupboard, the sixth under the wash-basin, the seventh into the case of the wall-clock.
sentences.i: The first sprang under the table, the second into the bed, the third into the oven, the fourth into the kitchen, the fifth into the cupboard, the sixth under the wash-basin, the seventh into the case of the wall-clock.
sentences.n: A single verb (sprang) governs the whole list — after the first clause German simply drops it and lets each hiding-place follow: “das zweite [sprang] ins Bett…” Note “das eine” = “the first (one)” here. The seventh’s hiding place, the clock-case, is the one that matters.

sentences.t: Aber der Wolf fand sie alle und machte nicht langes Federlesen: eins nach dem andern schluckte er in seinen Rachen; nur das jüngste in dem Uhrkasten das fand er nicht.
sentences.l: But the wolf found them all and made not long feather-picking: one after the other swallowed he into his maw; only the youngest in the clock-case that found he not.
sentences.i: But the wolf found them all and made no fuss about it: one after another he gulped them down his gullet; only the youngest, in the clock-case, he did not find.
sentences.n: “nicht (viel/langes) Federlesen machen” is an idiom — literally “to make no long feather-picking,” i.e. to make no fuss, waste no time. Rachen is a beast’s maw or gullet. The fronted “das” in “das fand er nicht” resumes “das jüngste” for emphasis. One survivor — as always, the smallest.

sentences.t: Als der Wolf seine Lust gebüßt hatte, trollte er sich fort, legte sich draußen auf der grünen Wiese unter einen Baum und fieng an zu schlafen.
sentences.l: When the wolf had sated his craving, trotted he himself away, laid himself outside on the green meadow under a tree and began to sleep.
sentences.i: When the wolf had sated his appetite, he trotted off, lay down outside on the green meadow under a tree, and began to sleep.
sentences.n: “seine Lust büßen” = to sate one’s craving, have one’s fill (here Lust = appetite, greed). “sich trollen” = to trot / clear off (reflexive, slightly contemptuous). anfangen (to begin) is separable, in the old spelling fieng an: “fieng an zu schlafen.” His full-bellied sleep is his undoing.

sentences.t: Nicht lange danach kam die alte Geis aus dem Walde wieder heim.
sentences.l: Not long thereafter came the old nanny-goat out of the forest again home.
sentences.i: Not long afterward the old goat came home again from the forest.
sentences.n: heimkommen (to come home) is separable, split here as “kam… heim.” “aus dem Walde” keeps an old dative -e on Wald (Walde), common in Grimm and now archaic.

sentences.t: Ach, was mußte sie da erblicken!
sentences.l: Ah, what had she there to catch-sight-of!
sentences.i: Oh, what a sight met her eyes!
sentences.n: erblicken = to catch sight of, behold. “was mußte sie da erblicken” is a rhetorical exclamation — literally “what she had to behold there!” — bracing the reader for the wreckage. mußte keeps the 1857 ß (modern musste).

sentences.t: Die Hausthüre stand sperrweit auf: Tisch, Stühle und Bänke waren umgeworfen, die Waschschüssel lag in Scherben, Decke und Kissen waren aus dem Bett gezogen.
sentences.l: The house-door stood wide open: table, chairs and benches were overturned, the wash-basin lay in shards, blanket and pillow were pulled out of the bed.
sentences.i: The front door stood wide open: table, chairs and benches overturned, the wash-basin lying in shards, blanket and pillow dragged out of the bed.
sentences.n: aufstehen here is separable and literal — “stand… auf” (stood open), with sperrweit “wide open” intensifying it. “in Scherben liegen” = to lie in pieces, be smashed. umwerfen (to knock over) gives the participle umgeworfen.

sentences.t: Sie suchte ihre Kinder, aber nirgend waren sie zu finden.
sentences.l: She sought her children, but nowhere were they to find.
sentences.i: She looked for her children, but they were nowhere to be found.
sentences.n: nirgend is the older form of modern nirgends (nowhere). “waren zu finden” = “were to be found”: sein + zu + infinitive expresses possibility, like English “to be found.”

sentences.t: Sie rief sie nacheinander bei Namen, aber niemand antwortete.
sentences.l: She called them one-after-another by name, but no one answered.
sentences.i: She called them one after another by name, but no one answered.
sentences.n: nacheinander = one after another. “bei Namen” = by name (Namen is the dative of der Name).

sentences.t: Endlich als sie an das jüngste kam, da rief eine feine Stimme „liebe Mutter, ich stecke im Uhrkasten.“
sentences.l: At last when she came to the youngest, then called a fine voice “dear mother, I stick in the clock-case.”
sentences.i: At last, when she came to the youngest, a thin little voice called: “Dear mother, I’m hiding in the clock-case.”
sentences.n: “ich stecke im Uhrkasten” — stecken here = to be stuck / tucked away inside. feine Stimme = a fine, thin voice — the tell that first betrayed the wolf now belongs to the one kid he never found.

sentences.t: Sie holte es heraus, und es erzählte ihr daß der Wolf gekommen wäre und die andern alle gefressen hätte.
sentences.l: She fetched it out, and it told her that the wolf had come and the others all had devoured.
sentences.i: She fetched it out, and it told her that the wolf had come and eaten all the others.
sentences.n: herausholen (to fetch out) is separable: “holte… heraus.” This is reported speech, so the verbs go into the subjunctive: “gekommen wäre… gefressen hätte” — German marks the kid’s account (as told, not as the narrator’s own fact) with this Konjunktiv, a pattern you’ll meet constantly in reported speech.

sentences.t: Da könnt ihr denken wie sie über ihre armen Kinder geweint hat.
sentences.l: There can you think how she over her poor children wept.
sentences.i: You can imagine how she wept over her poor children.
sentences.n: The narrator turns to address you directly — “Da könnt ihr denken” (“you can well imagine”). “über… weinen” = to weep over/for. könnt is the plain ihr-form (modern would add the -e: könnt/könnet).

sentences.t: Endlich gieng sie in ihrem Jammer hinaus, und das jüngste Geislein lief mit.
sentences.l: At last went she in her misery outside, and the youngest little-goat ran along.
sentences.i: At last, in her grief, she went outside, and the youngest kid ran along with her.
sentences.n: der Jammer = misery, lamentation. mitlaufen (to run along) is separable: “lief mit.” Again the old -ie- spelling gieng (went).

sentences.t: Als sie auf die Wiese kam, so lag da der Wolf an dem Baum und schnarchte daß die Äste zitterten.
sentences.l: When she came onto the meadow, then lay there the wolf by the tree and snored that the branches trembled.
sentences.i: When she came to the meadow, there lay the wolf by the tree, snoring so hard that the branches trembled.
sentences.n: A lovely hyperbole: “schnarchte, daß die Äste zitterten” — he snored so hard the branches shook. The daß-clause here is one of result (“so… that…”). die Wiese = the meadow.

sentences.t: Sie betrachtete ihn von allen Seiten, und sah daß in seinem angefüllten Bauch sich etwas regte und zappelte.
sentences.l: She looked-at him from all sides, and saw that in his filled-up belly something stirred and wriggled itself.
sentences.i: She looked him over from every side, and saw that something was stirring and wriggling in his stuffed belly.
sentences.n: betrachten = to look closely at, examine. angefüllt = filled up, stuffed (from anfüllen). “sich regen” = to stir, move (reflexive); zappeln = to wriggle, flounder. Her hope — that they were swallowed whole and alive — starts here.

sentences.t: „Ach Gott,“ dachte sie, „sollten meine armen Kinder, die er zum Abendbrot hinunter gewürgt hat, noch am Leben sein?“
sentences.l: “Ah God,” thought she, “should my poor children, whom he for supper down has choked, still on life be?”
sentences.i: “Oh God,” she thought, “could my poor children, whom he gulped down for his supper, still be alive?”
sentences.n: “sollten… noch am Leben sein?” — sollten here voices a wondering hope, “could they possibly still be…?” “am Leben sein” = to be alive. das Abendbrot = supper. hinunterwürgen = to choke/gulp down; würgen is a violent swallowing — Grimm doesn’t soften it.

sentences.t: Da mußte das Geislein nach Haus laufen und Scheere, Nadel und Zwirn holen.
sentences.l: Then had the little-goat to run home and fetch scissors, needle and thread.
sentences.i: So the little kid had to run home and fetch scissors, needle and thread.
sentences.n: Scheere is the 1857 spelling of modern Schere (scissors, shears). der Zwirn = twine, sewing thread. “nach Haus” = home(ward). The tools give away what’s coming: she means to open the wolf and sew him up again.

sentences.t: Dann schnitt sie dem Ungethüm den Wanst auf, und kaum hatte sie einen Schnitt gethan, so streckte schon ein Geislein den Kopf heraus, und als sie weiter schnitt, so sprangen nacheinander alle sechse heraus, und waren noch alle am Leben, und hatten nicht einmal Schaden gelitten, denn das Ungethüm hatte sie in der Gier ganz hinunter geschluckt.
sentences.l: Then cut she the monster the paunch open, and scarcely had she done a cut, then stretched already a little-goat the head out, and when she cut further, then sprang one-after-another all six out, and were still all alive, and had not even suffered harm, for the monster had them in its greed whole down swallowed.
sentences.i: Then she cut the monster’s belly open, and scarcely had she made one cut when a kid already poked its head out; and as she cut further, all six sprang out one after another, and were all still alive, and had not even come to harm, for the monster had gulped them down whole in its greed.
sentences.n: aufschneiden (to cut open) is separable: “schnitt… auf.” gethan is the 1857 spelling of getan (done), from thun. das Ungethüm (modern Ungetüm) = a monster, a huge brute; der Wanst = a beast’s (or fat) belly, paunch. “Schaden leiden” = to suffer harm (here gelitten, the participle). Because he bolted them whole in his greed (die Gier), they come out unhurt — the tale’s wish-fulfilment logic.

sentences.t: Das war eine Freude!
sentences.l: That was a joy!
sentences.i: What a joy that was!

sentences.t: Da herzten sie ihre liebe Mutter, und hüpften wie ein Schneider, der Hochzeit hält.
sentences.l: Then hugged they their dear mother, and hopped like a tailor who holds a wedding.
sentences.i: Then they hugged their dear mother and skipped about like a tailor at his own wedding.
sentences.n: herzen = to hug, embrace (from Herz, heart). “Hochzeit halten” = to hold / celebrate a wedding. “hüpfen wie ein Schneider, der Hochzeit hält” is a folk simile for hopping about with glee — tailors were proverbially nimble and merry.

sentences.t: Die Alte aber sagte „jetzt geht und sucht Wackersteine, damit wollen wir dem gottlosen Thier den Bauch füllen, so lange es noch im Schlafe liegt.“
sentences.l: The old one however said “now go and seek big-stones, with them will we the godless beast the belly fill, so long as it still in sleep lies.”
sentences.i: But the old goat said: “Now go and gather big stones — we’ll fill the godless beast’s belly with them while it’s still asleep.”
sentences.n: geht and sucht are plural imperatives (to the kids): “go and look for…” der Wackerstein = a big stone, a cobble. Thier is the 1857 spelling of Tier (animal, beast); gottlos = godless, wicked. “den Bauch füllen” — again the dative-of-possession style: “fill the belly to the beast,” i.e. its belly.

sentences.t: Da schleppten die sieben Geiserchen in aller Eile die Steine herbei und steckten sie ihm in den Bauch, so viel sie hinein bringen konnten.
sentences.l: Then dragged the seven little-goats in all haste the stones hither and stuck them to it in the belly, as many as they in bring could.
sentences.i: So the seven little goats dragged the stones over in all haste and stuffed them into its belly, as many as they could get in.
sentences.n: schleppen = to lug, drag. “in aller Eile” = in all haste. herbei = here, hither (toward the speaker). hineinbringen (to get inside) is separable, split here as “hinein… bringen.”

sentences.t: Dann nähte ihn die Alte in aller Geschwindigkeit wieder zu, daß er nichts merkte und sich nicht einmal regte.
sentences.l: Then sewed the old one him in all speed again shut, that he nothing noticed and himself not even stirred.
sentences.i: Then the old goat sewed him up again in all haste, so that he noticed nothing and didn’t even stir.
sentences.n: zunähen (to sew shut) is separable: “nähte… zu.” “in aller Geschwindigkeit” = at top speed. The result-clause daß (“so that he noticed nothing”) mirrors the daß of result you saw with the snoring.

sentences.t: Als der Wolf endlich ausgeschlafen hatte, machte er sich auf die Beine, und weil ihm die Steine im Magen so großen Durst erregten, so wollte er zu einem Brunnen gehen und trinken.
sentences.l: When the wolf finally had slept-his-fill, made he himself on the legs, and because the stones in the stomach aroused him such great thirst, then wanted he to a well go and drink.
sentences.i: When the wolf had finally slept his fill, he got to his feet; and because the stones in his stomach gave him such a great thirst, he decided to go to a well and drink.
sentences.n: ausschlafen (to sleep one’s fill) is separable: “ausgeschlafen hatte.” “sich auf die Beine machen” = to get to one’s feet, set off. “ihm… erregten” is once more a dative (“roused in him”): weil (because) sends its verb to the end (“erregten”).

sentences.t: Als er aber anfieng zu gehen und sich hin und her zu bewegen, so stießen die Steine in seinem Bauch aneinander und rappelten.
sentences.l: But when he began to go and to move himself to and fro, then knocked the stones in his belly against-one-another and rattled.
sentences.i: But when he began to walk and to sway to and fro, the stones in his belly knocked against one another and rattled.
sentences.n: anfangen appears again in the old spelling anfieng (“anfieng zu gehen”). “hin und her” = to and fro, back and forth. “aneinander stoßen” = to knock against one another; rappeln = to rattle, clatter — the sound sets up the rhyme.

sentences.t: Da rief er
sentences.l: Then called he
sentences.i: Then he cried out:
sentences.n: The sentence deliberately breaks off without a full stop: the little verse that follows completes it. After the fronted Da, the verb comes before the subject (“rief er”).

sentences.t:
„was rumpelt und pumpelt
in meinem Bauch herum?
ich meinte es wären sechs Geislein,
so sinds lauter Wackerstein.“
sentences.l:
“what rumbles and thumps
around in my belly?
I thought it were six little-goats,
but it’s all just big-stones.”
sentences.i: “What’s rumbling and thumping / round about in my belly? / I thought it was six little kids, / but it’s nothing but big stones.”
sentences.n: The wolf’s rhyme. rumpeln = to rumble, clatter; pumpeln is a nonce word coined to rhyme and thud alongside it (“thump, jolt”). “es wären” is subjunctive (“I thought it was…”, a supposed thing). sinds = sind es (“they are”), contracted; lauter here = “nothing but, sheer.” Wackerstein stays singular for the rhyme though he means the whole load of stones.

sentences.t: Und als er an den Brunnen kam und sich über das Wasser bückte und trinken wollte, da zogen ihn die schweren Steine hinein, und er mußte jämmerlich ersaufen.
sentences.l: And when he came to the well and bent himself over the water and wanted to drink, then pulled him the heavy stones in, and he had to miserably drown.
sentences.i: And when he came to the well and bent over the water to drink, the heavy stones dragged him in, and he had to drown miserably.
sentences.n: “sich bücken” = to bend down (reflexive). hineinziehen (to pull in) is separable: “zogen… hinein.” ersaufen is a coarse word for drowning, used of animals (a person would ertrinken) — Grimm keeps the low register for the beast. jämmerlich = miserably, wretchedly.

sentences.t: Als die sieben Geislein das sahen, da kamen sie herbei gelaufen, riefen laut „der Wolf ist todt! der Wolf ist todt!“ und tanzten mit ihrer Mutter vor Freude um den Brunnen herum.
sentences.l: When the seven little-goats saw that, then came they running hither, called loudly “the wolf is dead! the wolf is dead!” and danced with their mother for joy around the well round.
sentences.i: When the seven little goats saw it, they came running up, cried out loud, “The wolf is dead! The wolf is dead!” and danced round the well with their mother for joy.
sentences.n: todt is the 1857 spelling of tot (dead) — the old -dt, as in Noth, Roth. “kam gelaufen” = “came running” (a past participle after kommen expressing the manner of arrival). “um… herum” brackets the well (“all around the well”). “vor Freude” = for/out of joy.
